title: Entada tetraptera
taxonomy: Entada tetraptera is classified as a species of flowering plant in the pea family, Fabaceae. It is native to tropical Africa and Madagascar. The genus Entada is a part of the tribe Phaseoleae within the subfamily Faboideae. Entada tetraptera is further classified as a member of the section Macroptera. The plant's classification reflects its relationship to other plants in the pea family.
uses: Entada tetraptera is known to have medicinal properties, particularly in traditional medicine. Its seeds are said to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The plant is also used to treat various health issues, such as fever, rheumatism, and respiratory problems. Additionally, its bark and leaves are used to treat digestive issues and skin conditions. Further research is needed to confirm the extent of its medicinal uses.
benefits: Entada tetraptera has been traditionally used in folk medicine for various health purposes. The plant is believed to have anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. It may also help in reducing fever, alleviating cough, and treating respiratory issues. Furthermore, it has been used to treat digestive problems, such as diarrhea and constipation. However, more scientific research is needed to confirm the efficacy of Entada tetraptera for these uses.

parts: Entada tetraptera, commonly known as the cow itch tree, contains seeds that are used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The seeds are rich in alkaloids, which are believed to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. In traditional medicine, the seeds are used to treat conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and skin infections. side_effects: Entada tetraptera, a plant traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine, is not well-documented in modern medical literature regarding its side effects. However, it is known to be toxic to humans, containing compounds that can cause harm if ingested in large quantities. intro: Entada tetraptera, also known as the horse foot tree or elephant ear, is a plant species native to tropical regions of Asia and Australia, characterized by its unique, four-angled seed pods. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties that may aid in wound healing, reduce fever, and alleviate respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Entada tetraptera include flavonoids, terpenoids, and alkaloids, such as entadine and tetrapterine, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Entada tetraptera is often prepared as a decoction or infusions, and is used to treat various ailments, including fever, cough, and respiratory infections.
